name: Region-Based Memory
summary: Concept hub for memory-management techniques in which values are associated with regions whose lifetimes control allocation, access, or bulk reclamation; the design axis is *who specifies the region structure* (programmer, full inference, capability annotations, opt-in projection, runtime layout policy).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Region-based memory groups allocations by lifetime domain rather than reclaiming objects one by one. It offers a structured middle ground between tracing GC, reference counting, and strict ownership-only models.

The space covers both classic region systems — where lifetimes enable bulk deallocation — and newer region-like designs where regions act as isolation domains, borrowing/projection scopes, or allocator/runtime layout units. Compile-time RC and tracing GC sit at the two endpoints of "no annotation" approaches; regions are the structurally distinct middle ground.

sections.title: Design-space axes
sections.content:
The dominant axis across region designs is **who specifies the region structure**:

- **Programmer-specified regions**: explicit region variables on pointer types (Cyclone), explicit region annotations as concurrency isolation (Encore).
- **Full inference**: zero-annotation region inference (Tofte-Talpin / MLKit) at the cost of classic "stuck region" pathologies; ASAP-style fully automatic dataflow analysis with no GC fallback.
- **Capability annotations**: per-region reference capabilities (Verona's `iso`/`mut`/`imm`, Pony rcaps used as region transfers).
- **Opt-in projection**: regions only where the programmer wants zero-overhead immutability (Vale's region borrowing on top of generational references).
- **Runtime layout policy**: regions as a runtime allocator structure rather than a type-system feature (RC-Immix's mark-region heap, MLKit's per-region collectors).

Secondary axes: lexical vs non-lexical lifetimes, single-region per object vs. region-polymorphic functions, uniform heap cells vs. sized allocations.

sections.title: Regions as concurrency isolation
sections.content:
A modern strand uses regions as **isolation domains** rather than as a deallocation discipline. Verona makes every object live in exactly one of a forest of regions with one active mutable region per thread (Behaviour-Oriented Concurrency); Encore's reference-capability types control aliasing across active-object boundaries. The unifying design idea is that capabilities-as-regions can replace whole-program data-race avoidance with type-system-enforced unshared local heaps and capability-checked transfers.

Vale takes the opposite direction with regions as immutability projection: pure functions and `region` blocks temporarily make data immutable to elide generation checks, while the rest of the program runs on generational references. Per-region pluggable memory strategies (Verona) and group-borrowing (Vale's 2025 proposal) both relax the "one collector for the whole heap" assumption.

sections.title: Fully automatic, no-GC region-like analysis
sections.content: The most aggressive no-annotation, no-GC point in the design space is fully automatic dataflow analysis: the compiler emits per-program-point deallocation instructions, falling back to bounded runtime scans only when static info is insufficient. ASAP (Cambridge PhD 2017) is the canonical example, subsuming both linear types and Tofte-Talpin regions; given a linear/region-correct program, ASAP emits equivalent free instructions, and where static analysis can't decide it inserts specialised scan-and-deallocate code that is bounded (unlike a tracing GC).

sections.title: Non-lexical and hybrid region models
sections.content:
Modern region systems often relax strict lexical stack discipline and combine regions with borrowing, ownership, or tracing. The general lesson is that region systems are not limited to lexical stack-shaped lifetimes — Spegion explicitly proposes implicit, non-lexical, sized-allocation regions as the modern reformulation, and Boyapati-Salcianu-Beebee-Rinard fused ownership types with region types on RTSJ Java to eliminate runtime scoped-memory checks.

The hybrid story extends to allocator-runtime layer: RC-Immix uses Immix's mark-region heap as the substrate for reference counting, blurring "region" with "runtime block layout policy." The space is now an active design surface for languages searching for a middle point between GC, ownership, and arena allocation.

sections.title: Trade-offs and design implications
sections.content:
Classic region systems use lifetimes so many values can be reclaimed together, reducing per-object reclamation cost and making allocation extremely cheap when lifetime structure is predictable. The trade-off is annotation burden vs. inference power vs. expressiveness across concurrency and runtime implementation.

For language designers: (1) is the goal bulk deallocation (classic regions, arenas), isolation (Verona, Encore), or zero-overhead immutability projection (Vale)? (2) is the annotation cost acceptable, or is full inference required? (3) do regions interact with concurrency boundaries, and if so do they replace actor-style mailboxes or compose with them? (4) is a hybrid with tracing GC (MLKit + GC, RC-Immix, ASAP fallback) acceptable, or is regions-only a hard requirement?

sections.title: Per-technology records
sections.content:
Region-based mechanisms with their own per-technology records:

- **Explicit lexical regions**: Cyclone (the canonical "explicit regions, modest annotation cost" reference and direct ancestor of Rust lifetimes).
- **Inferred regions**: MLKit's region inference and the Tofte-Talpin region calculus that grounds it.
- **Modern non-lexical / sized-allocation regions**: Spegion (ECOOP 2025).
- **Regions as concurrency isolation**: Verona's per-region pluggable strategies; Encore's capability-checked active objects; Vale's opt-in region borrowing on top of generational references; Boyapati-Salcianu-Beebee-Rinard's RTSJ Java ownership+region fusion.
- **No-annotation, no-GC analysis**: ASAP's dataflow-driven deallocation insertion.
- **Runtime-layer region/RC hybrid**: RC-Immix's mark-region + reference-counted heap.
- **Cross-related concepts**: ownership and borrowing for the structurally adjacent disciplines; garbage collection for the contrasting reclamation strategy.
